video_title: How The Supreme Court is F**king up the Country
gist: Hasan Minhaj and UPenn constitutional law professor Kate Shaw argue the 6-3 Roberts Court has become a predictable partisan actor that is seizing lawmaking power from Congress and agencies (via overturning Chevron, the major questions and unitary-executive doctrines, and an exploding 'shadow docket'), repeatedly green-lighting Trump 2.0 executive actions without written reasoning, and gutting the Voting Rights Act. The signal for policy-exposed investors is sharply elevated legal/regulatory unpredictability across environmental, agency-funding, immigration, and redistricting matters.

themes.theme: Shadow docket: fast-track, unexplained rulings for the executive

insights.claim: Use of the Supreme Court's emergency (shadow) docket by the White House has surged from 8 requests across the 16 Bush-plus-Obama years to 41 in Trump's first term and over 30 in the first year of Trump's second term, signaling that emergency relief is now a routine governing tool rather than a rare exception.
insights.speaker: Hasan Minhaj
insights.so_what: Policy can now flip on weeks-long timelines via unexplained orders, compressing the window for markets to price legal/regulatory outcomes in policy-exposed sectors.
insights.quote: During the 16 years of the Bush and Obama presidencies, they appealed for emergency relief just eight times. But during Donald Trump's first term, the White House requested it 41 times. And in the first year of his second term, Trump 2.0, they requested it over 30 times.

insights.claim: The Roberts Court has ruled for the Trump administration on the shadow docket more than two dozen times in the past 16 months, reversing reasoned lower-court losses without issuing any written explanation.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: A high base rate of unexplained executive-favorable reversals raises the expected value of betting on Trump-administration policies surviving litigation, even where lower courts rule against them.
insights.quote: two dozen plus times in the past 16 months they have ruled ruled big in favor of the Trump administration in these cases where Trump loses below in some carefully reasoned lower court opinion

insights.claim: Shadow-docket orders have let the Trump administration proceed with third-country deportations (to places like South Sudan and Venezuela), removal of all transgender service members from the military, ethnicity-and-accent-based ICE stops, elimination of ~$4 billion in foreign aid, and dismantling over a third of the Department of Education despite lower-court blocks.
insights.speaker: Hasan Minhaj
insights.so_what: Federal funding streams (foreign aid contractors, education programs) can be terminated and survive challenge quickly, a tail risk for entities dependent on appropriated funds.

insights.claim: Shaw argues the court deliberately handles its most controversial executive-favorable rulings on the unexplained shadow docket precisely because the outcomes would be hard to defend in a written opinion.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Lack of written reasoning removes the precedential signposts investors and lawyers normally use to forecast future rulings, raising legal uncertainty.
insights.quote: it may not be a coincidence that it's some of the most nefarious stuff that happens in the least transparent way because I think they think this stuff would be hard to defend if you had to write an opinion explaining yourself, and so they don't.

themes.theme: Chevron's death and the agency-power vacuum

insights.claim: The Supreme Court overturned Chevron deference in 2024 (the 1984 Chevron v. NRDC standard), transferring the power to interpret ambiguous statutory terms from expert federal agencies to judges.
insights.speaker: Hasan Minhaj
entities: Chevron v. Natural Resources Defense Council
insights.so_what: Every agency rule resting on a debatable statutory reading is now more vulnerable to challenge, raising compliance and litigation risk for regulated industries and lowering the durability of new regulation.
insights.quote: this Supreme Court overturned that decision in 2024 and said judges should be the ones that decide what any vague language means

insights.claim: Shaw frames the major questions doctrine as an invented tool the court uses selectively to strike down expert-agency actions it dislikes, having deployed it against environmental regulations, student-loan forgiveness, and COVID mitigation measures.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Economically significant agency actions (emissions rules, debt relief, public-health mandates) face an extra, unpredictable doctrinal hurdle that favors industry-friendly outcomes.
insights.quote: they have used it to invalidate environmental regulations, student loan forgiveness, and COVID mitigation measures

insights.claim: Shaw says the Roberts Court resolves statutory ambiguity to favor industry interests, citing its willingness to strike down agency interpretations deemed too burdensome on coal companies' emissions cleanup.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Carbon/emissions and other environmental compliance costs may be lower than statute-on-paper implies, a structural tailwind for fossil-fuel and heavy-industry margins.
insights.quote: if we think that an interpretation of a statute is going to be too burdensome on industry, on coal companies, because it's going to require them to really clean up the emissions that they produce, nah, we're going to we're going to strike that interpretation

themes.theme: Unitary executive theory and agency funding risk

insights.claim: Shaw describes the supercharged unitary executive theory as letting the president unilaterally cancel billions in congressionally appropriated funds and dismantle agencies such as USAID, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, and the Department of Education.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Appropriations are no longer a reliable floor; counterparties, grantees, and regulated firms relying on a given agency's continued existence or funding face discrete cancellation risk.
insights.quote: he can decide to unilaterally cancel billions of dollars in appropriated funds, dismantle federal agencies

insights.claim: Shaw notes the doctrines are applied asymmetrically: unitary-executive power expands presidential control under Trump while the major questions doctrine was invoked under Biden to block EPA carbon regulation, OSHA rules, and student-loan forgiveness, making outcomes track the president's party rather than neutral principle.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Regulatory durability is now partly a function of which party controls the White House, so the same rule carries different survival odds across administrations.
insights.quote: When Trump's president, it's all unitary executive theory. But then when Biden is president, they're like, "No, no, no, no, the EPA can't regulate carbon emissions that way."

insights.claim: The court relied partly on the major questions doctrine to invalidate Trump's tariffs, a rare instance of the doctrine cutting against the administration that Shaw says hinged in part on economic and global-market disruption.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Severe market disruption can itself become a factor the court weighs, hinting tariff-type executive actions with broad economic fallout are more legally vulnerable than other Trump policies.
insights.quote: they they partly relied on major questions to invalidate the Trump tariffs

themes.theme: Voting Rights Act gutted and the redistricting free-for-all

insights.claim: Shaw says Louisiana v. Calais (decided weeks before taping) dealt a 'death blow' to the remaining Voting Rights Act, allowing maps that disadvantage Black voters so long as legislators frame the intent as partisan rather than racial.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Aggressive mid-cycle redistricting is now largely litigation-proof, shifting expected House control and raising the odds of durable single-party state-level governance that affects state fiscal and regulatory policy.
insights.quote: just a couple of weeks ago in Louisiana versus Calais, the Supreme Court largely deals a death blow to what remains of the Voting Rights Act

insights.claim: In the three weeks since Calais, Republican-controlled legislatures in Tennessee, Alabama, and Florida moved to eliminate or split majority-Black districts ahead of the midterms, with the court already clearing Alabama's map via a 6-3 shadow-docket order.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: A wave of pre-midterm redistricting is already underway, a near-term variable for handicapping 2026 House control and the legislative/fiscal agenda that follows.

insights.claim: Shaw argues the practical effect of the intent-only standard is that discriminatory actions with clear disparate impact go unchallenged because smoking-gun evidence of intent almost never exists, illustrated by a North Carolina legislature that requested race-of-voter data then cut the early-voting methods Black voters used most.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Legal recourse against outcome-discriminatory laws is now structurally weak, meaning aggressive state policies survive challenge and the status quo of GOP-favorable maps is more entrenched than headlines suggest.
insights.quote: intent requirement is going to mean that very, very few cases challenging what are actually discriminatory moves in practice, in their effect, those are going to just be able to you know, go unchallenged

themes.theme: A predictably partisan court and how it got there

insights.claim: Shaw dates the modern hyper-partisan court to Brett Kavanaugh replacing Anthony Kennedy in 2018, arguing it produced the first nine-justice bench in the modern era where each justice votes predictably with the policy preferences of the president who appointed them.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Outcomes can be forecast from the appointing president's party with unusual reliability, making the court a tractable variable to model in policy-driven trades.
insights.quote: nine justices, each of whom votes pretty predictably with the policy preferences of the presidents who appointed them. And that was not always the case.

insights.claim: Shaw asserts the court has effectively become a lawmaking body, invalidating and narrowly reading statutes via invented doctrines so that current governance is 'as much the product of the Supreme Court as Congress.'
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Legislative wins are no longer durable end-states; the court is a second veto/rewrite point, so policy risk persists well past a bill's passage.
insights.quote: It has in many ways taken onto itself the power to make laws. And in some ways we're living under a system that is as much the product of the Supreme Court as Congress

insights.claim: Shaw characterizes the Federalist Society as a half-century, strategically instrumental pipeline that cultivates originalist/textualist judges from their first year of law school and seeds supporting legal scholarship, now placing six of nine sitting justices.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: The conservative legal apparatus is durable and self-replenishing, so the current ideological tilt of the federal judiciary is likely a multi-decade regime rather than a cyclical swing.
insights.quote: have you said you you know, you support originalism, textualism, these kind of Federalist Society endorsed modes of interpretation

insights.claim: Shaw says there is no liberal counterpart to the Federalist Society because liberals reject its outcome-first, retrofit-the-argument approach, conceding it leaves the legal left at a strategic disadvantage that won't be quickly offset.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: The structural asymmetry means a future Democratic administration would need years to shift judicial outcomes, so the regulatory-skeptic tilt of courts is unlikely to reverse with one election.
insights.quote: fundamentally for better or worse, a lot of the time liberals don't think about the law in quite so instrumental a fashion

themes.theme: Birthright citizenship as a long-game wedge

insights.claim: Shaw expects Trump to lose the birthright-citizenship case but reads the litigation as a deliberate strategy to tally even two or three justices and shift the legal Overton window on a previously unthinkable challenge to the 14th Amendment.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Even losing test cases progressively normalize once-settled-law challenges, so 'settled' constitutional baselines investors rely on for long-horizon planning are less stable than assumed.
insights.quote: it has created some uncertainty, some doubt, some pathway by which some future president or future Congress could try again where I think that was unthinkable previously.

insights.claim: Shaw notes the court already ruled last year that lower courts cannot issue nationwide injunctions against the birthright-citizenship order, forcing challenges into the slower class-action format.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Removing nationwide injunctions lets contested executive actions take partial effect during litigation, lengthening the period of legal limbo for affected policies and the people/firms exposed to them.
insights.quote: this kind of procedural question of whether lower courts can issue something called nationwide injunctions. The court said no, they can't. This has to be a class action.

insights.claim: Shaw points out the Constitution fixes neither the number of justices (only that there be a chief) nor a term length, so Congress could by ordinary statute expand the court, add presidential appointments, impose 18-year term limits, or require written reasons for shadow-docket orders.
insights.speaker: guest, Kate Shaw (UPenn constitutional law professor)
insights.so_what: Court composition is a live, statute-level political variable; a future unified government could reshape it, a low-probability but high-impact tail that would re-rate every policy-sensitive position.
insights.quote: Congress could just pass a law changing the size of the Supreme Court. Could make it much bigger. Could give a president a bunch of additional appointments. We could do term limits.
